answer_key.q15: E. The commercial value of bees

passages.title: The Ecological Importance of Bees
passages.source_html:
A


Some time in the early Cretaceous period of the Earth's history, hunting wasps of a certain type became bees by adopting a vegetarian diet: they began to rely more and more on the pollen of plants as a source of protein for themselves and their offspring, as an alternative to insects. In so doing, they accidentally transported pollen on their bodies to other plants of the same species, bringing about pollination. The stage was thus set for a succession of ever-closer mutual adaptations of bees and flowering plants. In particular, flowers began to reward bees for their unwitting role in their reproduction by providing richer sources of pollen and another source of nutrition, nectar.


B


Today about 15 per cent of our diet consists of crops which are pollinated by bees. The meat and other animal products we consume are ultimately derived from bee-pollinated forage crops, and account for another 15 per cent. It follows that around one third of our food is directly or indirectly dependent on the pollinating services of bees. On a global basis, the annual value of agricultural crops dependent on the pollination services of bees is estimated at £1,000 million (US$1,590 million). Much of this pollination is due to honey bees, and in monetary terms it exceeds the value of the annual honey crop by a factor of fifty.


C The pursuit of self-interest


But the apparently harmonious relationship between bees and plants conceals a conflict of interests. Although flowers need bees and vice versa, it pays each partner to minimise its costs and maximise its profits. This may sound like an extreme case of attributing human qualities to non-human species, but using the marketplace and the principles of double-entry book keeping as metaphors may give us some insights into what is really going on between bees and flowering plants. In the real world, both flower and bee operate in a competitive marketplace. A community of retailers, the flowers, seek to attract more or less discriminating consumers, the bees. Each flower has to juggle the costs and benefits of investing in advertising, by colour and scent, and providing rewards, nectar and pollen. Clearly a species which depends on cross-pollination is on a knife-edge: it must provide sufficient nectar to attract the interest of a bee, but not enough to satisfy all of its needs in one visit. A satiated bee would return to its nest rather than visit another flower. The bee, on the other hand, is out to get the maximum amount of pollen and nectar. It must assess the quality and quantity of rewards which are on offer and juggle its energy costs so that it makes a calorific profit on each foraging trip. The apparent harmony between plants and bees is therefore not all that it seems. Instead, it is an equilibrium based on compromises between the competing interests of the protagonists.


D


This sounds remarkably like the ideas of the 18th-century economist Adam Smith. In his book, The Wealth of Nations, Smith postulated that in human society the competitive interactions of different 'economic units' eventually resulted in a balanced, or 'harmonious' society. One might predict, therefore, that economists would find the relationships between bees and plants of some interest. This is the case in Israel, where economists are collaborating with botanists and entomologists in a long-term study of the pollination biology of the native flora, in an attempt to understand the dynamics of the relationship between communities of bees and plants.


E


This sort of study is of more than passing academic interest. It is important that authorities understand the dynamic relationships between plants and their pollinators. This is especially true when, say, devising conservation policies. A good example comes from the forests of tropical South America. Here, as in all rainforests, there is a high diversity of tree species. There may be more than 120 per acre, but in a given acre there may only be one or two individuals of any one species: These trees are pollinated by large, fast-flying bees. There is evidence that certain types of bee learn the distribution of these scattered trees and forage regularly along the same routes. This is called 'trap-lining' and the bees forage for up to 23 km from their nests. The bees are therefore acting as long-distance pollinators.


F


An issue of current concern in tropical forest conservation is that of trying to estimate the minimum sustainable size of islands' forest reserve in areas where large-scale felling is taking place. There is much discussion on seed dispersal distances. But this is only one half of the equation, so far as the reproduction of trees is concerned. There is another question that must be addressed in order to calculate whether proposed forest reserves are close enough to the nearest large tract of forest: 'What is the flight range of these long-distance foragers?' We need to know much more about bees and their relationships with plants before this question can be answered.


G


Bees, then, are vital to our survival. Furthermore, much of the visual impact of human environments derives from vegetation, and most vegetation is dependent on bees for pollination. Thus, as pollinators of crops and natural vegetation, bees occupy key positions in the web of relationships which sustain the living architecture of our planet.

groups.title: Questions 14-26
groups.source_html:
Questions 14 – 18


The text has seven paragraphs. Choose the correct heading for paragraphs A, B, D, E, F and move it into the gap. The heading for one paragraph has already been selected.

Options (Word Bank):
A. Parallels between bee and human activities
B. An evolutionary turning point
C. A lack of total co-operation
D. The preservation of individual plant species
E. The commercial value of bees
F. The structure of flowering plants
G. The need for further research
14
正确答案： 14 B. An evolutionary turning point  |  15 E. The commercial value of bees  |  16 A. Parallels between bee and human activities   |  17 D. The preservation of individual plant species  |  18 G. The need for further research
Question 14

原文：

A段，“Sometime in the early Cretaceous period of the Earth's history, hunting wasps of a certain type became bees by adopting a vegetarian diet ”

解析：

从A段首句可知，白垩纪早期某种类型的狩猎黄蜂通过采用素食变成了蜜蜂，这就是进化转折点。

Question 15

原文：

B段，“Today about 15 per cent of our diet consists of crops which are pollinated by bees. The meat and other animal products we consume are ultimately derived from bee-pollinated forage crops, and account for another 15 per cent.”

解析：

B段开头说我们饮食中大约有15%是由蜜蜂授粉的农作物组成，我们消费的肉类和其他动物产品最终来自蜜蜂授粉的饲料作物，这表明我们的饮食消费和蜜蜂密切相关，蜜蜂具有很高的商业价值。

Question 16

原文：

D段，“Smith postulated that in human society the competitive interactions of different 'economic units' eventually resulted in a balanced, or 'harmonious'’ society. One might predict, therefore, that economists would find the relationships between bees and plants of some interest.”

解析：

原文D段提到了在人类社会中不同“经济单位”之间的竞争相互作用最终会导致一个平衡的或“和谐”的社会，因此有人可能会预测经济学家会对蜜蜂和植物之间的关系感兴趣，这就表明了人类活动与蜜蜂活动之间是有共通之处的。

同义替换：

calorific profit=calorific rewards

Question 17

原文：

E段，“It is important that authorities understand the dynamic relationships between plants and their pollinators. This is especially true when, say, devising conservation policies.”

解析：

原文E段提到了当局了解植物与其传粉者之间的动态关系是很重要的，尤其是在制订保护政策的时候，后文还举了南美热带地区森林的例子，此段内容与选项iv保存单个植物物种相吻合。

同义替换：

preservation=conservation

Question 18

原文：

F段，“We need to know much more about bees and their relationships with plants before this question can be answered.”

解析：

原文F段最后一句提到在回答这个问题之前，我们需要更多地了解蜜蜂以及它们与植物的关系，也就表明在未来我们需要做更多的研究以便于回答上面的问题。

Questions 19 - 25


Complete the sentences below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER in each gap.

19

19 Hunting wasps used to feed on other , rather than on vegetation.

正确答案： 19 insects
Question 19

原文：

A段，“they began to rely more and more on the pollen of plants as a source of protein for themselves and their offspring, as an alternative to insects.”


解析：

此题需要填hunting wasps过去是以什么为食，A段定位句说他们开始越来越依赖植物的花粉作为自己和后代的蛋白质来源，作为昆虫的替代品。由此可得，黄蜂在以植物花粉为食之前是吃其他昆虫的，所以此空答案为insects。


同义替换：

plants = vegetation

20

20 Flowering plants started to reward bees with rich pollen and an additional food in the form of .

正确答案： 20 nectar
Question 20

定位关键词：

flowering plants；rich pollen

原文：

A段，“In particular, flowers began to reward bees for their unwitting role in their reproduction by providing richer sources of pollen and another source of nutrition, nectar.”

解析：

此题需要填开花植物开始用丰富的花粉和哪种形式的额外食物奖励蜜蜂，A段最后一句讲了花朵开始通过提供更丰富的花粉来源和另一种营养来源花蜜来奖励蜜蜂在繁殖中不知不觉的作用。所以此空答案为nectar。

同义替换：

richer sources of pollen = rich pollen

another source of nutrition = additional food

21

21 Approximately of human food production relies on the activity of bees.

正确答案： 21 one third##1/3
Question 21

定位关键词：

human food production；activity of bees

原文：

B段，“It follows that around one third of our food is directly or indirectly dependent on the pollinating services of bees.”

解析：

此空可先预判为名词，并且是A of B结构，可以知道空格处内容肯定和human food production紧密相关，再根据定位处原文可知人类三分之一的食物直接或间接依赖于蜜蜂的授粉服务，所以此空为one third。

同义替换：

rely on=depend on

pollinating services of bees=activity of bees

22

22 If the process of is to take place effectively, bees need to travel from one flower to another before going back to the nest.

正确答案： 22 cross-pollination
Question 22

定位关键词：

travel one flower to another

原文：

C段，“Clearly a species which depends on cross-pollination is on a knife-edge: it must provide sufficient nectar to attract the interest of a bee, but not enough to satisfy all of its needs in one visit. A satiated bee would return to its nest rather than visit another flower.”

解析：

此空先预判要填一个表示蜜蜂从一朵花飞到另一朵花这一过程的名词，根据C段原文内容可知，这一过程是异花授粉（cross-pollination），花朵必须提供足够多的花蜜来吸引蜜蜂，这样才不至于让蜜蜂吃饱了会回到巢穴，而是在花朵之间来回飞以达到更加高效授粉的目的。

23

23 Bees need to balance the of each trip against the calorific rewards they obtain.

正确答案： 23 energy costs
Question 23

定位关键词：

each trip；calorific rewards

原文：

C段，“It must assess the quality and quantity of rewards which are on offer and juggle its energy costs so that it makes a calorific profit on each foraging trip.”

解析：

此题需要填一个与calorific rewards（热量奖励）相平衡的名词，原文C段指出蜜蜂必须评估所提供的奖励的质量和数量，并平衡其能量消耗，以便在每次觅食旅行中获得热量利润。所以此空答案为energy costs。

同义替换：

calorific profit=calorific rewards

24

24 There can be over 120 different in an acre of rainforest.

正确答案： 24 tree species
Question 24

定位关键词：

120；an acre of rainforest

原文：

E段，“ Here, as in all rainforests, there is a high diversity of tree species. There may be more than 120 per acre”

解析：

根据120很快定位到E段中间，原文提到南美热带雨林的树种种类繁多， 每英亩可能超过 120 个。此空填tree species。

同义替换：

diversity of tree species=different species

25

25 The bees that pollinate large forests regularly practise an activity known as .

正确答案： 25 trap-lining
Question 25

定位关键词：

regularly practise an activity

原文：

E段，“There is evidence that certain types of bees learn the distribution of these scattered trees and forage regularly along the same routes. This is called 'trap-lining' ”

解析：

此空要填在大森林授粉的蜜蜂经常要进行的活动名称，根据原文E段可知蜜蜂会定期沿着相同的路线觅食，这被称为“trap-lining”。

同义替换：

pollinate regularly=scattered trees and forage regularly

Questions 26


Choose the correct answer.

26

Which is the best title for Reading Passage 2?

正确答案： A. The Ecological Importance of Bees

Q26, A

原文：

G段，“Bees, then, are vital to our survival. Furthermore, much of the visual impact of human environments derives from vegetation, and most vegetation is dependent on bees for pollination. Thus, as pollinators of crops and natural vegetation, bees occupy key positions in the web of relationships which sustain the living architecture of our planet.”


解析：

最后一段提到蜜蜂对我们的生存至关重要，也在维持我们星球生命结构的关系网络中占据着关键地位，living architecture of our planet其实就是地球生态，所以跟选项A 的ecological importance一致。
